_meta.authoringNotes: Androgenetic alopecia epidemiology and treatment per Sinclair et al. Br J Dermatol 2018 and AAD/EADV joint position statements. Alopecia areata epidemiology and JAK-inhibitor data per Strazzulla et al. JAAD 2018 (PMID 29241771) and King et al. NEJM 2022 BRAVE-AA trial (PMID 35334197). Telogen effluvium and scarring alopecia per Olsen EA et al. JAAD 2003 NAHRS workshop. Hair-transplant outcomes per Avram MR et al. and ISHRS practice census 2022. ICD-10 codes per WHO classification (L63 alopecia areata, L64 androgenetic, L65 telogen effluvium, L66 scarring/cicatricial). Finasteride and dutasteride dosing per FDA label and Mella et al. Arch Dermatol 2010 meta-analysis.

h1Title: Alopecia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Alopecia is the medical term for hair loss and covers a family of distinct conditions ranging from common androgenetic (male- or female-pattern) thinning to autoimmune alopecia areata, stress-driven telogen effluvium, and scarring inflammatory disorders. Roughly half of men show visible hair loss by age 50 and 40% of women by age 50, while alopecia areata affects about 2% of people at some point in life with peak onset under age 30. The diagnostic question is not whether hair is being lost but which pattern is occurring, whether the follicle is still alive, and what is driving it — answers that determine whether treatment will restore visible regrowth or only stabilize current density. The 2022 approval of oral JAK inhibitors baricitinib and ritlecitinib for severe alopecia areata transformed treatment of the autoimmune form; minoxidil, finasteride, spironolactone, low-level laser therapy, platelet-rich plasma, and follicular-unit-excision transplantation address the much larger androgenetic group. Early recognition of scarring alopecia is the highest-impact diagnostic call: once follicles are destroyed regrowth is impossible.

keyStats.prevalence: Androgenetic alopecia affects approximately 50% of men by age 50 and 40% of women by age 50; alopecia areata lifetime prevalence around 2%
keyStats.demographics: All ethnicities affected; central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia disproportionately affects Black women; frontal fibrosing alopecia rising in postmenopausal women
keyStats.avgAge: Androgenetic alopecia typically begins age 20-40; alopecia areata onset most commonly under 30; telogen effluvium any age
keyStats.globalCases: Hair loss is one of the top dermatology consultations worldwide; the global hair-care and hair-loss treatment market exceeded USD 8 billion in 2023

definition: Alopecia (ICD-10: L63, L64, L65, L66) is a broad term for partial or complete loss of hair from areas where it normally grows. Clinically and biologically the condition is split into non-scarring alopecia, in which the follicular ostia and stem cells remain intact and regrowth is biologically possible, and scarring (cicatricial) alopecia, in which the follicle is destroyed and replaced by fibrosis, making regrowth impossible. Within non-scarring alopecia, androgenetic alopecia (L64) reflects genetically programmed follicular miniaturization driven by dihydrotestosterone in androgen-sensitive scalp regions; alopecia areata (L63) is a T-cell-mediated autoimmune attack on the anagen hair bulb; telogen effluvium (L65.0) is a synchronized shift of follicles into the resting telogen phase triggered by a systemic stressor (illness, childbirth, surgery, weight loss, thyroid dysfunction, drugs); anagen effluvium (L65.1) is loss of actively growing hairs from chemotherapy, radiation, or toxin exposure. Scarring alopecias are subdivided by histology into lymphocytic (lichen planopilaris, frontal fibrosing alopecia, central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia, discoid lupus erythematosus), neutrophilic (folliculitis decalvans, dissecting cellulitis), and mixed forms. Trichoscopy, scalp biopsy, and targeted laboratory testing distinguish these patterns. The condition is managed by dermatology, with endocrinology, rheumatology, and psychiatry input where systemic disease or psychological impact is relevant.

typesClassification.type: Androgenetic alopecia (male-pattern and female-pattern hair loss)
typesClassification.description: Progressive miniaturization of follicles in androgen-sensitive scalp regions. In men: bitemporal recession and crown thinning (Hamilton-Norwood pattern). In women: diffuse thinning over the crown with preservation of the frontal hairline (Ludwig pattern). Cumulative effect of genetic susceptibility and dihydrotestosterone.

Autoimmune T-cell attack on anagen hair bulbs. Presents as discrete round or oval patches of complete hair loss with preserved follicular ostia and characteristic exclamation-mark hairs at the edge. Severe forms: alopecia totalis (whole scalp), alopecia universalis (whole body), and ophiasis (band along occipital and temporal hairline).

Diffuse shedding of 100-300 hairs daily, 2-4 months after a triggering event (febrile illness, childbirth, major surgery, rapid weight loss, iron deficiency, thyroid disease, severe psychological stress, or certain drugs). Self-limited; resolves within 6-12 months once the trigger is corrected.

Loss of actively growing anagen hairs days to weeks after exposure to a toxic insult — most often cytotoxic chemotherapy, radiation, severe protein malnutrition, or thallium poisoning. Affects 65-100% of the scalp depending on the agent.

Permanent destruction of follicles by inflammation and fibrosis. Subtypes: lichen planopilaris (perifollicular erythema and scale, scalp pruritus), frontal fibrosing alopecia (band-like recession of frontal hairline with eyebrow loss in postmenopausal women), central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia (crown-centered scarring most common in Black women), discoid lupus erythematosus, folliculitis decalvans, and dissecting cellulitis.

Mechanical follicle injury from sustained hair tension — tight braids, weaves, extensions, ponytails, religious turban-wearing, helmet use. Initially reversible; chronic traction over years produces scarring.

Compulsive hair pulling producing patchy hair loss of variable length and asymmetric shape, distinct from alopecia areata by broken hairs of differing lengths within affected zones. Often coexists with anxiety, depression, or OCD.

primarySymptoms: Gradual recession of the frontal hairline at the temples and progressive thinning over the crown in men — the classic Hamilton-Norwood male-pattern progression spanning 10-30 years.
Diffuse widening of the central parting and reduced ponytail volume without true bitemporal recession in women — the Ludwig female-pattern presentation.

Sudden appearance of one or more well-circumscribed coin-shaped patches of complete hair loss with preserved follicular openings and characteristic exclamation-mark hairs at the patch border — alopecia areata.

Diffuse heavy shedding (100-300 hairs daily for weeks, particularly noticeable on the shower drain or pillow) starting 2-4 months after a triggering illness, childbirth, surgery, weight loss, or stress — telogen effluvium.
Rapid global hair loss within 1-3 weeks of chemotherapy initiation — anagen effluvium.
Progressive recession of the frontal and temporal hairline with thinning of the eyebrows and visible perifollicular erythema in postmenopausal women — frontal fibrosing alopecia.
Painful, itchy, or burning scalp with red, scaly, or pustular patches of hair loss and loss of follicular ostia visible on dermoscopy — scarring alopecia.
Crown-centered patches of broken hair, perifollicular hyperpigmentation, and scarring in middle-aged Black women — central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia.
Patchy hair loss along the hairline or scalp from tight braiding, weaves, or sustained traction — early traction alopecia, with little hairs along the affected border (fringe sign).
Loss of eyebrows, eyelashes, and body hair in addition to scalp involvement — alopecia universalis or chemotherapy-induced anagen effluvium.
Visible nail pitting, trachyonychia, or fingernail ridging — present in 10-66% of patients with alopecia areata.
earlyWarningSigns: Increased hair on the pillow, shower drain, or hairbrush over several weeks
Subtle widening of the central scalp parting noticed when styling hair
Reduced ponytail circumference compared with the previous year
Solitary smooth round patch of complete hair loss on the scalp, beard, or eyebrow
Itchy, burning, or tender scalp accompanied by hair shedding — red flag for scarring alopecia
emergencySigns: Sudden, painful, ulcerating scalp lesions with rapid hair loss — exclude infection, vasculitis, or cutaneous malignancy
Rapidly progressive frontal hairline recession with scaling, erythema, or eyebrow loss — likely scarring alopecia requiring biopsy
Hair loss accompanied by malar rash, joint pain, or sun sensitivity — exclude systemic lupus erythematosus
Profound shedding with weight loss, palpitations, or temperature intolerance — exclude hyperthyroidism or other endocrine cause
Patches of hair loss with broken hairs of different lengths in a child or adolescent with anxiety or stress — consider trichotillomania and assess mental-health risk

causes.description: Polygenic inheritance — over 200 risk loci identified, with the AR gene on the X chromosome carrying the largest effect. In affected follicles, type II 5-alpha-reductase converts testosterone to dihydrotestosterone, which binds androgen receptors and progressively miniaturizes follicles over years.

causes.cause: Autoimmune T-cell attack on the hair follicle (alopecia areata)
causes.description: CD8+ T cells and natural killer cells infiltrate the anagen hair bulb and disrupt the immune-privilege normally protective of growing follicles. Strong associations with HLA-DQB1 alleles and shared susceptibility with vitiligo, autoimmune thyroid disease, type 1 diabetes, and atopic conditions.

causes.cause: Systemic stressor synchronizing follicles into telogen (telogen effluvium)
causes.description: Major physiological or psychological stressors push a substantial fraction of anagen follicles into the resting telogen phase simultaneously; mass shedding occurs 2-4 months later when those follicles are pushed out by the next anagen cycle. Triggers include febrile illness, childbirth, major surgery, rapid weight loss, iron and zinc deficiency, thyroid dysfunction, and severe emotional stress.

Cytotoxic chemotherapy and head radiation cause anagen effluvium. Many other drugs cause telogen effluvium: retinoids, ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, anticoagulants, anticonvulsants, antithyroid agents, isotretinoin, lithium, and hormonal contraceptive changes. Effect appears 2-4 months after initiation or dose change and resolves after withdrawal.

causes.cause: Mechanical hair tension and physical trauma (traction alopecia)
causes.description: Sustained tension on the hair shaft from tight braiding, weaves, ponytails, religious turbans, or helmet use. Causes early follicular damage that is reversible if traction is released; chronic traction over years produces scarring around hair follicles.

causes.cause: Primary scalp inflammation and fibrosis (scarring alopecias)
causes.description: Heterogeneous group: lymphocytic-mediated lichen planopilaris and frontal fibrosing alopecia, neutrophil-driven folliculitis decalvans, and mixed forms. Triggers include autoimmune disease, contact sensitizers (sunscreens implicated in frontal fibrosing alopecia), genetic predisposition, and chronic hair-care practices.

Iron deficiency, vitamin D insufficiency, zinc deficiency, severe protein malnutrition, biotin deficiency, and uncontrolled thyroid disease all impair anagen and provoke shedding. Effect is largely on telogen effluvium and worsening of androgenetic alopecia rather than a unique alopecia pattern.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of pattern hair loss
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of men with androgenetic alopecia have a 2.5-5× increased risk; concordance in monozygotic twins exceeds 80%. Female-pattern hair loss is also strongly heritable but with lower penetrance.
Cumulative prevalence of androgenetic alopecia rises steadily across decades: roughly 16% of men aged 18-29 and 53% aged 40-49 show measurable hair loss in epidemiological surveys.

Men reach more advanced stages on average than women because of higher circulating androgens. Female-pattern hair loss accelerates after menopause when estrogen falls and the androgen-to-estrogen ratio rises.

riskFactors.factor: Atopic background and autoimmune family history
riskFactors.description: Personal or family history of atopic dermatitis, asthma, vitiligo, thyroid autoimmunity, type 1 diabetes, or alopecia areata raises risk of alopecia areata. Roughly 25% of patients have a positive family history.

riskFactors.factor: Major life stressor in past 6 months
riskFactors.description: Febrile illness, childbirth, surgery, bereavement, severe weight loss, or sustained psychological stress are well-established triggers of telogen effluvium and may flare alopecia areata. The shed typically peaks 2-4 months after the trigger.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic hair-care practices with traction or chemical processing
riskFactors.description: Tight braids, weaves, extensions, ponytails, frequent chemical relaxers, and high-heat styling are well-documented contributors to traction alopecia and central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia. Risk is highest in Black women.

Thyroid disease, lupus, iron-deficiency anemia, polycystic ovary syndrome, vitamin D insufficiency, and uncontrolled diabetes are routinely associated with shedding and worsening androgenetic alopecia; correcting them often improves hair density.

affectedDemographics: Men: approximately 50% by age 50 show androgenetic alopecia; 80% by age 70.
Women: approximately 40% by age 50 show female-pattern hair loss; prevalence rises after menopause.
Alopecia areata: lifetime risk roughly 2% globally; half of cases begin before age 30.
Frontal fibrosing alopecia: rising sharply in postmenopausal women; estimated tenfold increase in incidence over the past 20 years.
Central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia: affects roughly 6-15% of Black women and is the most common cause of scarring alopecia in this group.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis starts with a focused history (onset, distribution, shedding versus thinning, triggers, family history, hair-care practices, drugs, comorbidities) and visual assessment of the scalp pattern. The clinician compares regions and looks for preserved or lost follicular ostia — the single most important sign separating non-scarring from scarring alopecia. A hair-pull test (gently extracting 30-60 hairs from three scalp regions) helps quantify active shedding: more than 6 telogen hairs per pull is abnormal. Trichoscopy (handheld polarized dermoscopy of the scalp) is now considered standard and demonstrates pattern-specific features: hair shaft diameter variation and miniaturization in androgenetic alopecia, exclamation-mark hairs and yellow dots in alopecia areata, perifollicular erythema and absent follicular ostia in scarring alopecia. Targeted laboratory testing includes complete blood count, ferritin (target above 70 ng/mL), TSH, vitamin D, and zinc; ANA, ENA, and androgen panels are added when systemic or hormonal disease is suspected. A 4 mm punch biopsy of the active edge — read by a dermatopathologist trained in horizontal sectioning — is the diagnostic standard for scarring alopecia and any presentation that cannot be classified clinically. Photography for serial comparison and validated severity scales (Sinclair score for female-pattern, SALT score for alopecia areata) anchor follow-up assessment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualizes hair shaft variability, miniaturization, exclamation-mark hairs, yellow and black dots, perifollicular signs, and presence or absence of follicular ostia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless examination with a handheld dermatoscope, completed in 5-10 minutes during the consultation. Result immediate; pattern-specific findings guide diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies active shedding by counting hairs extracted with gentle traction
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 60 hairs are gently grasped and pulled from three scalp regions. More than 6 telogen hairs per pull suggests active shedding. Painless; takes under 2 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Scalp punch biopsy (4 mm, horizontal sectioning)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnostic standard for scarring alopecia and unclassified cases; differentiates non-scarring from scarring and identifies specific subtype
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient procedure under local anesthesia. The clinician removes a small disc of scalp tissue, closes with 1-2 sutures, and submits for horizontal sectioning. Sutures out at 7-10 days; histology report in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Targeted blood tests (ferritin, TSH, T4, vitamin D, complete blood count, zinc, ANA where indicated)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects reversible systemic contributors and excludes underlying autoimmune or endocrine disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results within 24-72 hours. Ferritin under 70 ng/mL, TSH abnormalities, and vitamin D under 30 ng/mL are frequently identified contributors.

diagnosticTests.test: Androgen panel (free testosterone, DHEAS, prolactin, 17-OH-progesterone) in women
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovary syndrome, or rare adrenal sources of androgens in women with hirsutism, irregular cycles, or sudden hair loss
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw, ideally in the early follicular phase. Results within 1 week guide endocrinology referral.

diagnosticTests.test: Photographic documentation with standardized lighting and angles
diagnosticTests.purpose: Tracks progression and treatment response over months and years
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standardized photos taken at the consultation and at follow-up visits (typically every 4-6 months). Allows objective comparison rather than relying on subjective perception.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is matched to the specific alopecia subtype and patient priorities (slowing progression, regrowing visible hair, restoring eyebrows, or restoring confidence). For androgenetic alopecia, the cornerstones are topical minoxidil 2% or 5% twice daily (or once-daily 5% foam) and oral finasteride 1 mg daily in men or oral spironolactone 100-200 mg daily in women; oral minoxidil 0.625-5 mg daily is increasingly used off-label with strong real-world data and is now considered first-line by many specialists. Dutasteride is more potent than finasteride and used when finasteride fails, particularly in countries where it is approved for hair loss (South Korea, Japan). Combined treatment outperforms monotherapy and produces visible regrowth in 60-80% of patients within 6-12 months. Low-level laser therapy with FDA-cleared devices delivers modest density improvement. Platelet-rich plasma injections every 4-6 weeks for three sessions, then every 3-6 months, increase hair count by approximately 15-25% in placebo-controlled trials. Follicular-unit excision and follicular-unit transplantation are surgical options for stable, advanced cases. For alopecia areata, oral JAK inhibitors baricitinib (2-4 mg daily, FDA approved 2022) and ritlecitinib (50 mg daily, FDA approved 2023) produce ≥80% scalp regrowth (SALT-80) in roughly 35-40% of severe patients after 36-52 weeks. Intralesional corticosteroid injections (triamcinolone 2.5-10 mg/mL every 4-6 weeks) remain first-line for patchy disease. Topical and oral corticosteroids, topical immunotherapy with diphencyprone, methotrexate, and ciclosporin are alternatives. Telogen effluvium is managed by identifying and correcting the trigger; hair density usually recovers within 6-12 months. Scarring alopecias require early biopsy-driven diagnosis and prompt anti-inflammatory therapy (hydroxychloroquine, doxycycline, oral retinoids, intralesional steroids, methotrexate, JAK inhibitors in emerging data) to halt progression — the follicles that have already scarred cannot be recovered. Hair transplantation can restore stable areas of scarring once disease activity is quiet for at least 1-2 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Minoxidil 5% topical (twice daily) or 5% foam (once daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for androgenetic alopecia and a useful adjunct in alopecia areata and telogen effluvium. Prolongs anagen and increases follicle size; mechanism involves potassium-channel opening and prostaglandin pathway modulation. Initial shed of 4-8 weeks is expected.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Increased hair count by 18% and visible improvement in approximately 60% of users at 48 weeks (Olsen JAAD 2002 and subsequent trials)

medicalTreatments.name: Oral minoxidil 0.625-5 mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Off-label but increasingly first-line for both androgenetic alopecia and telogen effluvium. Better adherence and efficacy than topical in many patients; common side-effects include hypertrichosis on the face and body, fluid retention, and tachycardia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Hair count increases of 12-15% at 6 months in published series; high real-world satisfaction

medicalTreatments.name: Finasteride 1 mg orally daily (men)
medicalTreatments.description: 5-alpha-reductase type II inhibitor that reduces scalp DHT by ~60%. Slows progression in 90% of men and produces visible regrowth in roughly 65% over 2 years. Sexual side-effects occur in 1-2%; rare reports of persistent symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mean increase of 86 hairs/cm² (10%) at 1 year vs decline of 21 hairs/cm² on placebo (Mella Arch Dermatol 2010 meta-analysis)

medicalTreatments.name: Spironolactone 100-200 mg orally daily (women)
medicalTreatments.description: Aldosterone antagonist with peripheral androgen-receptor blockade; first-line for female-pattern hair loss especially with hirsutism or acne. Monitor potassium and blood pressure; teratogenic — contraceptive cover required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilization or improvement in 60-75% of women at 12 months in observational series

medicalTreatments.name: Baricitinib 2-4 mg orally daily (alopecia areata)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor — first FDA-approved systemic therapy for severe alopecia areata (2022). Targets the cytokine signaling driving autoimmune follicular attack. Monitor for infection, lipids, and cardiovascular risk; pre-treatment infection screen required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ≥80% scalp regrowth (SALT-80) in 35-39% at 36 weeks in BRAVE-AA1/AA2 (King NEJM 2022, PMID 35334197)

medicalTreatments.name: Ritlecitinib 50 mg orally daily (alopecia areata, ages 12+)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral JAK3 / TEC-family kinase inhibitor — FDA approved 2023 for severe alopecia areata in patients aged 12 and older. Pediatric data uniquely available; safety monitoring similar to baricitinib.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: SALT score ≤20 in 23% at week 24 in ALLEGRO trial (Olsen Lancet 2023)

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional triamcinolone (2.5-10 mg/mL) injections
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for patchy alopecia areata. Injected at 1 cm intervals into the dermis of active patches; repeated every 4-6 weeks. Risk of local atrophy if doses or frequency excessive.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Regrowth within 4-8 weeks in 60-80% of patches; less effective in extensive or rapidly progressive disease

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine 200-400 mg orally daily (scarring alopecia)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line systemic agent for lichen planopilaris, frontal fibrosing alopecia, and discoid lupus erythematosus of the scalp. Anti-inflammatory action stabilizes disease and reduces symptoms; annual ophthalmologic monitoring required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilization of disease activity in 60-70% of patients within 6-12 months; not regrowing — preventing further loss

surgicalOptions.name: Follicular-unit excision (FUE) hair transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Individual follicular units are harvested from the safe donor area at the back of the scalp using a 0.8-1.0 mm punch and reimplanted into thinning recipient sites. Day-case procedure under local anesthesia. Most popular modality globally since 2010.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft survival 85-95% at 12 months in experienced hands; visible cosmetic improvement in over 90% of well-selected patients

surgicalOptions.name: Follicular-unit transplantation (FUT, strip excision)
surgicalOptions.description: Linear strip of donor scalp is removed, dissected into individual follicular units under microscope, and reimplanted. Allows higher graft yield per session; leaves a linear scar.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft survival 90-95%; total hair recovery typically requires 1-3 sessions

surgicalOptions.name: Scalp reduction or flap surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Excision of bald scalp and advancement of hair-bearing scalp; reserved for highly selected cases of stable, advanced loss with adequate donor density. Largely superseded by transplantation but retains a role in burn and scar reconstruction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Useful for selected reconstruction cases; high satisfaction in carefully selected individuals

surgicalOptions.name: Eyebrow and beard transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Single-follicle FUE grafts placed at correct angle and direction to restore eyebrows lost to frontal fibrosing alopecia, alopecia universalis, or trauma.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Graft survival 70-85%; aesthetic outcome dependent on operator skill

alternativeTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) scalp injections
alternativeTreatments.description: Autologous PRP injected into scalp at 4-6 week intervals for 3-4 sessions, then maintenance every 3-6 months. Active growth factors stimulate follicular activity and prolong anagen. Meta-analyses show modest but reproducible benefit in androgenetic alopecia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-level laser therapy (LLLT) devices
alternativeTreatments.description: FDA-cleared red-light combs and helmets used 3 times weekly. Improve hair count by approximately 15-20% in pooled trial data of androgenetic alopecia; effect is gradual over 6-12 months.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist confirms the precise alopecia subtype with trichoscopy and, when indicated, scalp biopsy; identifies treatable systemic contributors; and selects evidence-based therapy tailored to subtype and severity. Specialist referral is essential before invasive treatments such as hair transplantation, which fails if applied to ongoing autoimmune or scarring disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a board-certified dermatologist with a specific hair-disorders interest, ideally trained at an academic alopecia clinic and familiar with current JAK-inhibitor protocols. For surgery, look for an ISHRS-affiliated hair-transplant surgeon with documented before-and-after results in your hair type and pattern. Avoid clinics that promise guaranteed regrowth or that recommend transplantation before subtype diagnosis.
hospitalCriteria: Dermatopathology service experienced in horizontal scalp sections
Access to JAK inhibitors and standardized infection screening
ISHRS-affiliated hair restoration surgery if transplantation is planned
Onsite endocrinology and rheumatology for systemic contributors
keyFacilities: Dedicated hair and scalp clinics within academic dermatology centres
Dermatopathology services with horizontal scalp section expertise
Reproductive endocrinology services for women with hyperandrogenism
Mental-health services for body-image and trichotillomania support
preventionStrategies: Avoid sustained tight hair tension (braids, weaves, ponytails worn the same way daily, religious turbans without padding) and rotate styles to reduce traction injury.
Use sulfate-free shampoos and avoid frequent chemical processing (relaxers, perms) in those prone to scarring alopecia.
Treat dandruff and seborrheic dermatitis early to limit scalp inflammation that exacerbates shedding.
Maintain ferritin above 70 ng/mL, vitamin D above 30 ng/mL, and a euthyroid state to support normal hair cycling.
Discuss hair-related side-effects before starting drugs known to provoke telogen effluvium (retinoids, anticoagulants, anticonvulsants, ACE inhibitors).
lifestyleModifications: Treat scalp pruritus and inflammation early — recurrent itch or pain predicts scarring alopecia activity.
Apply minoxidil on a dry scalp once or twice daily, exactly as prescribed; expect 4-8 weeks of mild initial shed.
Photograph the scalp every 3-4 months under standardized lighting for objective comparison.
Limit heat styling above 175 °C and avoid pulling wet hair tightly.
Manage stress, sleep, and mental health — recurrent stress is a documented trigger for telogen effluvium and alopecia areata flares.
Wear sunscreen on bald or thinning scalp areas to prevent ultraviolet damage and reduce skin-cancer risk.

recommended: Iron-rich foods (lean red meat, liver, lentils, dark leafy greens) with vitamin C to support ferritin
Adequate protein intake (1-1.2 g/kg/day) to support keratin synthesis
Omega-3-rich foods (salmon, sardines, flaxseed) with documented anti-inflammatory benefit on scalp
Zinc-containing foods (oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef) where dietary intake is low
avoid: Severely restrictive crash diets and very low-calorie regimens, which routinely trigger telogen effluvium
High-dose vitamin A and isotretinoin without medical supervision, which can provoke effluvium
High-dose selenium and biotin supplements unless deficient — biotin interferes with thyroid and troponin assays
Smoking and excessive alcohol, both linked to worsened androgenetic alopecia in cohort studies

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week) improves stress and metabolic markers that influence shedding. Resistance training does not increase androgenic alopecia risk in men despite older claims about testosterone increases. Wear absorbent headbands rather than tight caps to reduce sweat retention and scalp irritation.
dailyManagement: Apply prescribed topical minoxidil on a dry scalp at fixed times.
Use a wide-tooth comb and avoid aggressive towel-drying on wet hair.
Take oral medications (finasteride, spironolactone, baricitinib) with the same daily routine to improve adherence.
Track shedding in a brief log — date, severity, and any triggers.
Apply broad-spectrum sunscreen on bald or thinning scalp before outdoor exposure.
Attend follow-up photography and density check every 4-6 months.
prognosis: Outlook depends on subtype, severity, and treatment timing. Androgenetic alopecia is progressive without treatment; with optimal therapy started early, more than 80% of men halt visible progression and 60-65% achieve cosmetically meaningful regrowth, and similar proportions of women stabilize or improve. Alopecia areata has a more variable course — roughly 30-50% of patchy cases regrow spontaneously within a year, but extensive (≥50% scalp) or long-standing disease has lower spontaneous remission rates and historically poor response to standard treatments; JAK inhibitors have improved outcomes substantially, with 35-40% achieving near-complete regrowth by 36-52 weeks. Telogen effluvium has an excellent prognosis with trigger correction; density returns to baseline within 6-12 months in 90% of cases. Scarring alopecias have the most guarded outlook: areas already destroyed cannot regrow, but timely diagnosis and treatment can arrest progression in 60-75% of patients. Outcomes in central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia and frontal fibrosing alopecia are best when treatment starts in early disease before significant follicular loss. Hair transplantation outcomes are durable in stable androgenetic alopecia (graft survival 85-95% at 1 year), provided ongoing medical therapy is continued to preserve native hair.
recoveryTimeline: Topical minoxidil: initial shed weeks 4-8, visible improvement months 4-6, plateau by 12 months. Oral finasteride: stabilization by 6 months, visible regrowth by 12 months in responders. JAK inhibitors for alopecia areata: initial regrowth by 12 weeks, maximum effect by 36-52 weeks. Telogen effluvium: resolution within 6-12 months of trigger correction. Hair transplantation: grafted hairs shed by week 4, regrow from month 3-4, final density by month 12-18. Scarring alopecias: stabilization within 6-12 months of effective therapy; existing density not recoverable.
complications: Permanent follicular loss and irreversible scarring if scarring alopecia is diagnosed late.
Psychological morbidity — depression, anxiety, social withdrawal, and reduced quality of life are commonly reported in moderate-to-severe alopecia; consider mental-health support early.
Scalp dermatitis, allergic contact dermatitis to minoxidil, or contact dermatitis to immunotherapy treatments.
Drug-related side-effects (sexual dysfunction with finasteride, hyperkalemia or breast tenderness with spironolactone, infections and lipid changes with JAK inhibitors).
Donor-area scarring or visible thinning following high-density hair transplantation when donor capacity is overestimated.
Persistent post-treatment shedding when underlying drivers (iron deficiency, thyroid disease, mental-health stress) remain unaddressed.

supportResources.name: National Alopecia Areata Foundation (NAAF)
supportResources.description: Largest patient organisation for alopecia areata — education, support groups, advocacy, and research updates.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Hair Loss Resources
supportResources.description: Patient-facing guidance from the leading US dermatology professional body, including treatment and self-care advice.

supportResources.name: International Society of Hair Restoration Surgery (ISHRS)
supportResources.description: Professional body for hair restoration surgeons with a verified directory of accredited members and consumer guides.

supportResources.name: Alopecia UK
supportResources.description: UK-based charity providing patient support, peer groups, and clinical-trial information for all forms of alopecia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tinea capitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tinea capitis (fungal scalp infection) causes scaly, often inflammatory patches with broken hairs, kerion formation, and positive KOH or fungal culture. Alopecia areata patches are smooth and non-scaly with preserved follicular ostia and negative fungal studies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trichotillomania
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trichotillomania is hair-pulling behavior producing patches of broken hairs of varying lengths within the affected zone and bizarre, asymmetric shapes; trichoscopy shows V-sign, flame hairs, and broken hairs. Alopecia areata patches are smooth and uniformly hairless with exclamation-mark hairs at the border.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Discoid lupus erythematosus of the scalp produces atrophic, hyperpigmented or hypopigmented plaques with scarring alopecia and absent follicular ostia. Diagnosed by biopsy showing interface dermatitis and immune-complex deposition. Alopecia areata is non-scarring and lacks lupus serologies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Scalp psoriasis produces thick, silvery, well-demarcated plaques with shedding but rarely true alopecia; hair returns when plaques are treated. Alopecia areata patches are smooth and complete without scale.

relatedConditions.name: Vitiligo
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune disease sharing immune-privilege loss; co-occurs with alopecia areata in 4-12% of cases and responds to similar JAK-inhibitor therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Common autoimmune skin disease often involving the scalp; commonly differentiated from alopecia areata in the dermatology clinic.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: Atopic background is associated with alopecia areata; severe atopic dermatitis on the scalp can mimic non-scarring alopecia.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus
relatedConditions.relevance: Discoid and systemic lupus produce scarring alopecia and must be excluded before treating presumed alopecia areata.

relatedConditions.name: Underactive Thyroid
relatedConditions.relevance: Common reversible contributor to telogen effluvium and worsening of androgenetic alopecia; screening TSH is standard.

faqs.question: What is alopecia?
faqs.answer: Alopecia is the medical term for hair loss. It is not a single disease but a family of conditions ranging from common pattern hair loss (androgenetic alopecia) to autoimmune patchy hair loss (alopecia areata), stress-related shedding (telogen effluvium), and scarring inflammatory disorders. Treatment depends on which type is present.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of hair loss?
faqs.answer: Androgenetic alopecia is by far the most common cause, affecting roughly half of men and 40% of women by age 50. It is a genetically programmed, gradual miniaturization of hair follicles driven by dihydrotestosterone, producing receding hairlines and crown thinning in men and diffuse central thinning in women.

faqs.question: Is alopecia areata an autoimmune disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Alopecia areata is caused by T cells attacking the hair follicle and disrupting the immune privilege that normally protects growing hairs. It is associated with other autoimmune conditions including vitiligo, autoimmune thyroid disease, and type 1 diabetes, and runs in families in roughly 25% of cases.

faqs.question: Can alopecia be cured?
faqs.answer: Most forms can be effectively treated but not permanently cured. Androgenetic alopecia is stabilized or improved by minoxidil and finasteride or spironolactone in most patients. Alopecia areata can achieve substantial regrowth with JAK inhibitors. Scarring alopecia can be arrested but already destroyed follicles cannot grow back.

faqs.question: How quickly does alopecia treatment work?
faqs.answer: Visible benefit from minoxidil and finasteride typically appears at 4-6 months and plateaus by 12 months. Intralesional steroid injections for alopecia areata patches show regrowth in 4-8 weeks. Oral JAK inhibitors achieve maximum regrowth between weeks 36 and 52. Telogen effluvium recovers within 6-12 months of trigger correction.

faqs.question: What is telogen effluvium?
faqs.answer: Telogen effluvium is a temporary diffuse hair shedding that begins 2-4 months after a major trigger — a febrile illness, childbirth, surgery, sudden weight loss, iron deficiency, thyroid problems, or severe stress. Up to 300 hairs are shed daily. It is self-limited and resolves within 6-12 months once the trigger is corrected.

faqs.question: Is hair loss a normal part of aging?
faqs.answer: Some degree of hair miniaturization is universal with age, but visible hair loss is not inevitable. Androgenetic alopecia is the major driver, with cumulative prevalence rising in each decade. Lifestyle, hormones, nutrition, and underlying disease all modify the rate; effective treatments exist to slow or reverse progression.

faqs.question: Does stress cause hair loss?
faqs.answer: Severe physical or psychological stress can trigger telogen effluvium, with diffuse shedding starting 2-4 months later. Chronic stress can also flare alopecia areata and worsen androgenetic alopecia. Most stress-related shedding is reversible once the stressor is addressed and underlying contributors (iron, thyroid, sleep) are corrected.

faqs.question: What is finasteride and is it safe?
faqs.answer: Finasteride is an oral medication that blocks the enzyme converting testosterone to dihydrotestosterone, reducing scalp DHT by roughly 60%. It slows progression in 90% of men and produces regrowth in around 65% over 2 years. Sexual side-effects occur in 1-2% and are usually reversible; rare persistent symptoms are reported. Not approved in women of reproductive age.

faqs.question: Does minoxidil really work?
faqs.answer: Yes. Topical and oral minoxidil increase hair count and visible density in roughly 60% of users with androgenetic alopecia within 6-12 months. An initial 4-8 week shedding phase is normal and predicts future response. Effect is maintained only with continued use.

faqs.question: What are JAK inhibitors for alopecia areata?
faqs.answer: JAK inhibitors are oral immunomodulators that block cytokine signaling driving the autoimmune attack on hair follicles. Baricitinib (FDA approved 2022) and ritlecitinib (2023) produce ≥80% scalp regrowth in 35-40% of severe alopecia areata patients within 36-52 weeks and have transformed treatment of the autoimmune form.

faqs.question: Can hair transplants give permanent results?
faqs.answer: Transplanted hairs from the genetically resistant donor area at the back of the scalp retain their resistance to DHT and tend to persist for life. Graft survival is 85-95% at 12 months in skilled hands. Underlying androgenetic alopecia still progresses, so medical therapy is usually continued to preserve native hair around the grafts.

faqs.question: Does PRP work for hair loss?
faqs.answer: Platelet-rich plasma injections produce modest but real improvement in androgenetic alopecia in placebo-controlled trials, with hair count increases of approximately 15-25%. Typical protocol is three or four sessions at 4-6 week intervals followed by 6-month maintenance. Best as an adjunct rather than monotherapy.

faqs.question: Can children get alopecia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Alopecia areata commonly begins in childhood; tinea capitis is the most common cause of patchy hair loss in children. Trichotillomania appears in school-age and adolescent children. Androgenetic alopecia can begin in adolescence in those with strong genetic loading.

faqs.question: What tests should I have for hair loss?
faqs.answer: Standard workup includes complete blood count, ferritin, TSH, vitamin D, and zinc. Women with sudden onset, irregular periods, or signs of hyperandrogenism should add free testosterone, DHEAS, and prolactin. A scalp biopsy is added when scarring alopecia is suspected or when the pattern is unclear after trichoscopy.

faqs.question: Does diet affect hair loss?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially through micronutrient deficiencies. Iron deficiency, low vitamin D, zinc deficiency, and severely restricted diets all worsen shedding. Adequate protein, iron, omega-3, and zinc support normal hair growth. Most supplements offer minimal benefit unless a deficiency is documented; biotin in particular is rarely deficient and can interfere with thyroid testing.

faqs.question: Why is frontal fibrosing alopecia becoming more common?
faqs.answer: Incidence has risen approximately tenfold over the past 20 years, especially in postmenopausal women. The cause is not fully understood but proposed contributors include hormonal changes, leave-on sunscreens, and possibly facial cosmetics. Early diagnosis and combination treatment with hydroxychloroquine and 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors arrest progression in most patients.

faqs.question: Is scarring alopecia reversible?
faqs.answer: No. Once a follicle has been destroyed by inflammatory scarring it cannot regrow. The aim of treatment is to stop further loss; existing density can be improved cosmetically with transplantation only after disease activity has been quiet for at least 1-2 years.

faqs.question: Will my hair grow back after chemotherapy?
faqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Chemotherapy-induced anagen effluvium typically reverses within 3-6 months of completing treatment, with full density returning by 12 months. Texture and color may differ initially. Scalp cooling devices during infusion reduce hair loss in roughly 50% of patients.

faqs.question: Does wearing hats cause baldness?
faqs.answer: No. Hats and helmets do not cause androgenetic alopecia. Very tight hats worn for long hours can contribute to traction alopecia at the hairline in susceptible individuals, but standard hat use is not a cause. Hereditary, hormonal, and autoimmune factors drive most hair loss.

faqs.question: Can hair loss be reversed naturally?
faqs.answer: Reversible causes — telogen effluvium from illness, deficiency, stress, or drug effects — usually recover spontaneously once the trigger is corrected. Androgenetic alopecia does not reverse without active medical treatment. Lifestyle factors (sleep, nutrition, stress management) support medical therapy but do not replace it for genetic or autoimmune forms.

metaDescription: Alopecia (hair loss): pattern baldness, alopecia areata, telogen effluvium and scarring forms. Diagnosis, minoxidil, finasteride, JAK inhibitors, transplant.
